metadata.title:
MRS. STAFFORD AND PRESS
REPORTER VISIT THE


CITY JAIL

pages.raw_text:
This 10 q truthful picture Of city
Jail conditions in Spokane, as seen
by Mrs. p. p. Stafford, head of the
police matron committee of the
Wonan's club, and a reporter for
The Press. The visit was made one
day last week, while the alleged
police investigation by Mayor
Pratts partisan committee was in
progress. In view Of the impending
whitewash, it was deemed advisable
to call at the Jail and see how
things really were at that time, the
supposition being that conditions
would to great extent be anchor
ated because Of the police being I'M
der fire.


Chief Of Police Sullivan was alb
sent when Mrs. Stafford and the re-
porter called and application was
made to Captain Weir at the desk
for permission to enter. One of the
women in the Jail had previously
sent word to Mrs. Stafford through
reporter that she wanted to see
her, and in response to this request
the application for admission was
made. The officers at the desk
granted the request and Jailer Mel
son unlocked the doors to the
prison.


The lower cells were first in
spected, showing the exposed bath
tub for the women, bare of all priv
acy. There was one woman in the
lower cell who complained of her
treatment, and said that she had be
come covered with vermin, and that
the place was generally horrible"
to use her own words


Then the inspection was directed
to the second floor, where four
women were confined. One woman
washed blankets during the morn
ing until she had made herself i'll
and was lying on the inside cot, I'M
able to get up. She had washed the
blankets to prevent getting body
lice, that the women said infested
the place.


One of the women complained of
being i'll, and looked really sick. She
said that she had called for atten-
ton from the city physician when
he passed through the Jail. but that
his reply was, "Ah, get out; You're
all right This woman was under
a doctors care when arrested.


The girls complained Of the food.
and one plate, containing the pid-
day meal, served for the Ack
woman. but left untouched by Her.
was exhibited. This dinner" con-
sisted of a piece Of sole leather
beefsteak. fried so hard and dry
that laborer in a construction gang
could not digest it, There were two
potatoes, that had a sun burned
weatherbeaten appearance, as! if
they might have done service on
some former occasions. This, with
two pieces of bread, composed the
menu for which the taxpayers of
Spokane had paid Is cents. Its du-
plicate could be turned out in any
cheap restaurant for six or eight
cents.


The odor about the place was
